content.full_text:
October 26, 1989


Gary Kunkel
Committee to Elect Gary S. Kunkel
to San Luis Obispo City Council
P.O. Box 3135
San Luis Obispo, CA  93403

                                        Re:  Your Request for Advice
                                             Our File No. I-89-598

Dear Mr. Kunkel:

     We acknowledge receipt of your letter in which you seek
advice from the Commission concerning a provision of the San Luis
Obispo City Election Campaign Regulation, No. 2.40.050.C.6.

     The Commission is authorized by statute to provide advice
concerning a person's obligations under the Political Reform Act
(Government Code Section 83114(b)).¹  We have no jurisdiction with
respect to, and cannot provide interpretations of, local
ordinances.  We suggest that you contact your city attorney for
advice concerning this or any other of the city's regulations.

     I am sorry that we cannot be of any further assistance to you
on this matter.

                                        Sincerely,


                                        Kathryn E. Donovan
                                        General Counsel

                              By: Jonathan S. Rothman
                                  Counsel, Legal Division

KED:JSR:plh




1  Government Code Sections 81000-91015.


428 J Street, Suite 800  •  P.O. Box 807  •  Sacramento CA 95804-0807  •  (916) 322-5660

COMMITTEE TO ELECT GARY S. KUNKEL TO SAN LUIS OBISPO CITY COUNCIL
P.O. BOX 3135, SAN LUIS OBISPO, CALIFORNIA 93403 (805)543-5480

Enforcement Division
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. Box 807
Sacramento, CA 95804-0807


Subject: Request for ruling concerning Legality/Fairness of San Luis Obispo
City, Election Campaign Regulation 2.40.050.C.6.


Dear Enforcement Division:

The San Luis Obispo City Election Campaign Regulations 2.40.050.C6 states
in part: "No person shall make or accept any contribution in excess of ten
dollars after five p.m., Tuesday, seven days preceding the day of election,....."

This provision is in clear violation of the stated "Purpose and Intent" of the
regulation: "To promote integrity, honesty and fairness in municipal election
campaigns".

The obvious result of this provision is that it handicaps a challenger, such
as myself, during the most critical part of the campaign. Other candidates,
that are associated with established political organizations, simply file
their nomination papers, push a few buttons, and out comes a large amount
of money to run their campaigns. In contrast, an independent candidate,
such as myself, will become most popular after the public forums, during
the last two weeks of the campaign. In past elections, highly qualified
candidates have even had to return donation checks that were dated before
the cut-off date, because they arrived in the mail after the cut-off date.
This obviously amplifies the power of special-interest groups while it
handicaps the campaigns of independent candidates.

Another significant point, is that the $10.00 amount is an arbitrarily low
amount. It is far below the disclosure amount that requires disclosure of
"the name, address, and amount of the contribution for each person
contributing $25 or more." If the purpose of the "seven-day" cutoff is only
to allow ample time for voter review of contributor lists, then why $10.00,
why not $24.99. For example, if some one can donate $15, $20, or $24.99
before the last week without having to give their name, address, etc., why
should the same person be denied that right after the seven-day cut-off
period. It doesn't make sense to me, unless the regulation was purposely
designed to handicap independent challengers, such as myself.

My last point is that the seven-day cut-off period, corresponding to a filing
requirement date, seems somewhat arbitrary. Instead of Tuesday why not
make filing requirements on Friday @ 12:00. This would still give ample
time before the election for disclosure information to be published by the
news media or by any other interested person(s). It would also give
candidates a greater opportunity to use more radio, TV, and newspaper
advertisement during the last and most crucial week of the campaign.

In conclusion, the $10.00/seven-day limitation is unfair, and it violates
principles of fair/equal representation for all citizens. Since i am
considering accepting at least one donation over $10.00, (in order to
challenge this ordinance in court, if need be.), please make a ruling
concerning the legality of this ordinance as soon as possible. Also it is my
hope that you will address the issue in a timely manner so that I can get on
with the important tasks at hand.


Sincerely,

[signature]
Gary S. Munkel


P.S. Please feel free to call me for more information and/or details.

COMMITTEE TO ELECT GARY S. KUNKEL TO SAN LUIS OBISPO CITY COUNCIL
P.O. BOX 3135, SAN LUIS OBISPO, CALIFORNIA 93403 (605) 543-5480

Secretary of State
Political Reform Division
1230 J. Street
Sacramento, CA 95814


Subject: Request for ruling concerning Legality/Fairness of San Luis Obispo
City, Election Campaign Regulation 2.40.050.C.6.


Dear Political Reform Division:

The San Luis Obispo City Election Campaign Regulations 2.40.050.C6 states
in part: "No person shall make or accept any contribution in excess of ten
dollars after five p.m., Tuesday, seven days preceding the day of election,…"

This provision is in clear violation of the stated "Purpose and Intent" of the
regulation: "To promote integrity, honesty and fairness in municipal election
campaigns".

The obvious result of this provision is that it handicaps a challenger, such
as myself, during the most critical part of the campaign. Other candidates,
that are associated with established political organizations, simply file
their nomination papers, push a few buttons, and out comes a large amount
of money to run their campaigns. In contrast, an independent candidate,
such as myself, will become most popular after the public forums, during
the last two weeks of the campaign. In past elections, highly qualified
candidates have even had to return donation checks that were dated before
the cut-off date, because they arrived in the mail after the cut-off date.
This obviously amplifies the power of special-interest groups while it
handicaps the campaigns of independent candidates.

Another significant point, is that the $10.00 amount is an arbitrarily low
amount. It is far below the disclosure amount that requires disclosure of
"the name, address, and amount of the contribution for each person

to allow ample time for voter review of contributor lists, then why $10.00,
why not $24.99. For example, if some one can donate $15, $20, or $24.99
before the last week without having to give their name, address, etc., why

period. It doesn't make sense to me, unless the regulation was purposely

My last point is that the seven-day cut-off period, corresponding to a filing
requirement date, seems somewhat arbitrary. Instead of Tuesday why not

In conclusion, the $10.00/seven-day limitation is unfair, and it violates
principles of fair/equal representation for all citizens. Since I am
considering accepting at least one donation over $10.00, (In order to
challenge this ordinance in court, if need be.), please make a ruling
concerning the legality of this ordinance as soon as possible. Also it is my
hope that you will address the issue in a timely manner so that I can get on
with the important tasks at hand.


Sincerely,

Gary S. Kunkel

P.S. Please feel free to call me for more information and/or details.

sections.conclusion: The Commission is authorized by statute to provide advice concerning a person's obligations under the Political Reform Act (Government Code Section 83114(b)). We have no jurisdiction with respect to, and cannot provide interpretations of, local ordinances. We suggest that you contact your city attorney for advice concerning this or any other of the city's regulations.
sections.question_synthetic: Does the San Luis Obispo City Election Campaign Regulation 2.40.050.C.6, which prohibits contributions exceeding $10 after five p.m. on the Tuesday seven days before election day, violate fair election principles and unfairly handicap independent candidates?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC lacks jurisdiction to interpret local ordinances and can only advise on obligations under the state Political Reform Act; the requester should contact the city attorney for interpretation of San Luis Obispo's local campaign regulations.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document contains both incoming request (from Kunkel) and FPPC response. Extracted FPPC response data. No formal QUESTION or CONCLUSION headers present but content clearly identifies both sections. Minor character inconsistency in signature spelling (Kunkel vs Kunkei) noted in source document.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Does the San Luis Obispo City Election Campaign Regulation 2.40.050.C.6, which prohibits contributions exceeding $10 after five p.m. on the Tuesday seven days before election day, violate fair election principles and unfairly handicap independent candidates?

CONCLUSION: The Commission is authorized by statute to provide advice concerning a person's obligations under the Political Reform Act (Government Code Section 83114(b)). We have no jurisdiction with respect to, and cannot provide interpretations of, local ordinances. We suggest that you contact your city attorney for advice concerning this or any other of the city's regulations.

embedding.summary: The FPPC declined to provide advice on whether a San Luis Obispo city campaign regulation limiting late contributions to $10 was legal or fair, stating it lacks jurisdiction over local ordinances and recommending the requester consult the city attorney.
